sections.title: 2- The need to prioritise effective enforcement of existing rules rather than adding new obligations for traders
path: 2- The need to prioritise effective enforcement of existing rules rather than adding new obligations for traders

sections.title: 3- strengthening support for professionals to enable better application of existing rules
path: 3- strengthening support for professionals to enable better application of existing rules

paragraphs.text: Movement de Entreprises & de France

Taking note of the conclusions of the European Commission's Digital Fairness Fitness Check of 3 October 2024, MEDEF takes note of the Commission's commitment to develop online consumer protection rules with regard to:
Unethical techniques and commercial practices related to the design of manipulative or dark patterns (dark patterns, unfair personalisation practices).
Misleading marketing practices of influencers on social media.
Addictive design of certain digital products and online profiling in particular when consumer vulnerabilities are exploited for commercial purposes.
Difficulties in managing digital contracts in particular as regards subscriptions and subscriptions.

The framing of deceptive patterns that can put consumers in a vulnerable position is indeed essential, as consumer confidence is a lever for growth for e-commerce professionals. MEDEF thus supports the idea of ensuring fairness for consumers and businesses in transactions within the Digital Single Market, improving legal certainty, ensuring effective enforcement and preventing market fragmentation.

However, it should be stressed that this digital fairness cannot result from the introduction of new obligations for businesses. The current European legislation, which is very dense and complex, already offers a high level of consumer protection.
Thus, MEDEF wishes to raise the following points:
1- The importance of harmonisation at European level of the applicable rules on digital fairness:

The provision of digital services and content in the context of the development of e-commerce has, by its very nature, a cross-border dimension, with consumers increasingly using online shopping. For several years, EU Member States have taken national initiatives to regulate certain online practices that are considered unfair, while there is currently a European framework dealing with such practices. The Digital Fitness Check has also highlighted market fragmentation as Member States regulate or plan to regulate in these areas.

The multiplication of national regulations leads to legal uncertainty within the Union and uncertainty about the compliance of national practices, applicable penalties and competent supervisory authorities. Ultimately, this translates into a significant increase in compliance costs for economic operators active in several markets. This also creates barriers to cross-border trade and promotes unfair competition from non-EU actors that are not subject to the same regulatory requirements.

According to MEDEF, the Commission's objective must be a genuine European harmonisation of the applicable rules on digital fairness, ensuring both legal certainty and consumer protection. This means above all removing grey areas and regulatory overlaps. For example, the European Data Protection
MEDEF contribution — Draft Digital Fairness Regulation 23 October 2025

Board (EDPS) has recently demonstrated that targeted clarification, without resorting to new legislation, makes it possible to achieve concrete results. Indeed, its Guidelines 3/2025 of 11 September 2025 on the interaction between the Digital Services Act (DSA) and the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) have made it possible to fill the gaps and enhance the effectiveness of the existing legal framework, without adding additional regulation to the system. This pragmatic approach confirms that agile regulation, based on precise guidelines, is often more appropriate than new regulation.

On the other hand, creating a new legislative instrument would add unnecessary burden, add to the complexity of the already extremely dense existing framework and undermine the competitiveness of French and European businesses.

section_path: 2- The need to prioritise effective enforcement of existing rules rather than adding new obligations for traders
paragraphs.text: It is important to recall that European consumers are now among the most protected in the world thanks to regulatory developments over the last fifty years, with European digital legislation being particularly protective. While some risks related to misleading or abusive practices online remain, they relate, above all, to a lack of effective and homogeneous implementation of existing rules and controls.

section_path: 2- The need to prioritise effective enforcement of existing rules rather than adding new obligations for traders
paragraphs.text: Several European instruments already provide a comprehensive response to the problems identified by the Digital Fairness Fitness Check:

section_path: 2- The need to prioritise effective enforcement of existing rules rather than adding new obligations for traders
paragraphs.text: Directive 2005/29/EC of 11 May 2005 concerning unfair commercial practices (UTPs), as amended by Directive EU 2019/2161 of 27 November 2019, prohibits practices which are contrary to the requirements of professional diligence and which materially distort or are likely to materially distort the economic behaviour of the consumer. Its provisions make it possible, for example, to penalise practices of highlighting false information that may mislead the average consumer, misleading omissions, or aggressive practices (harassment, coercion, undue influence on the consumer). Annex I also provides numerous examples of practices deemed unfair in all circumstances, which makes it possible to grasp almost all of the digital fairness challenges highlighted by the Digital Fairness Fitness Check. In addition, the Directive adopts a basic approach which should be retained. According to the logic of this basic approach, a commercial practice is not unfair in nature and unfairness is a matter of how the trader implements it. The only exception to this approach is the exhaustive list of practices considered unfair in all circumstances, annexed to the Directive.

section_path: 2- The need to prioritise effective enforcement of existing rules rather than adding new obligations for traders
paragraphs.text: Regulation (EU) No 2016/679 of 27 April 2016 on data protection (GDPR) regulates operations relating to personal data, imposing conditions of lawfulness, fairness and transparency as regards collection and processing. The GDPR strictly regulates the processing of certain categories of data and establishes national data protection authorities. For example, the EDPS, as part of his guidelines on automated individual decision-making and profiling for the purposes of Regulation 2016/679, clarified that organisations must refrain from profiling children for marketing purposes. The GDPR also provides for requirements on consumer consent and information, including on consumer rights.

section_path: 2- The need to prioritise effective enforcement of existing rules rather than adding new obligations for traders
paragraphs.text: Where the UCPD and the GDPR are not intended to apply, Regulation (EU) No 2022/2065 of 19 October 2022 on the Single Market for Digital Services (DSA) makes it possible to prohibit operators of online platforms and online marketplaces (in particular Article 25) from using patterns that are misleading or that would affect or impede the ability of consumers to make free and informed decisions. That general prohibition of dark patterns is accompanied by an overall definition of what is capable of constituting such practices, provided for in recital 67 of that regulation.

section_path: 2- The need to prioritise effective enforcement of existing rules rather than adding new obligations for traders
paragraphs.text: Regulation (EU) No 2022/1925 of 14 September 2022 on digital markets (DMA) regulates the practices of providers of core platform services, so-called gatekeepers, by imposing on them several ex ante obligations that cover various practices under the new Digital Fairness Regulation: facilitation of dissubscription, prohibition of self-preference, management of the use of personal data for targeted advertising without explicit consent.

section_path: 2- The need to prioritise effective enforcement of existing rules rather than adding new obligations for traders
paragraphs.text: Regulation (EU) No 2024/1689 of 13 June 2024 laying down harmonised rules as regards artificial intelligence (AI Act), there are provisions (in particular Article 5) to combat misleading commercial practices and manipulative techniques based on artificial intelligence (SIA) systems. For example, the IA Act prohibits SIAs exploiting vulnerabilities of specific groups (age, disability, etc.) to distort their behaviour and which can cause significant harm; or SIAs that use

section_path: 2- The need to prioritise effective enforcement of existing rules rather than adding new obligations for traders
paragraphs.text: subliminal or manipulative techniques to materially distort a person's behaviour by impairing his or her ability to make an informed decision. By imposing specific transparency obligations towards users when interacting with AI (chatbot, deepfake, etc.), the text also strengthens consumer protection against such practices.

section_path: 2- The need to prioritise effective enforcement of existing rules rather than adding new obligations for traders
paragraphs.text: ► Finally, Directive 2023/2673/EU of 22 November 2023 amending Directive 2011/83/EU as regards financial services contracts concluded at a distance and repealing Directive 2002/65/EC on distance selling of financial services lays down an obligation for Member States to introduce measures restricting the use of dark patterns marketing techniques. This general ban on dark patterns for financial services contracts concluded at a distance is accompanied by a list of practices of traders considered misleading.

section_path: 2- The need to prioritise effective enforcement of existing rules rather than adding new obligations for traders
paragraphs.text: In this context, traders should not be subject to new obligations that would further complicate the applicable law. Strict enforcement of existing rules that have the merit of covering all identified cases is an optimal guarantee of consumer protection in the digital environment, without the need for new rules that could undermine legal certainty and the competitiveness of European businesses.

section_path: 3- strengthening support for professionals to enable better application of existing rules
paragraphs.text: In addition to the challenges of harmonisation and effective application of the rules, some economic operators now find it difficult to identify precisely which practices could be classified as unfair. Increased support would therefore be useful to clarify what is a misleading interface design and what constitutes a fair and compliant choice architecture.

section_path: 3- strengthening support for professionals to enable better application of existing rules
paragraphs.text: In this connection, MEDEF is in favour of setting up practical support mechanisms, such as the publication of guidelines by the European Commission to facilitate the implementation of the obligations already imposed on professionals, and the organisation of workshops bringing together all the stakeholders (supervisory authorities, businesses, professional organisations, consumer associations, etc.).

section_path: 3- strengthening support for professionals to enable better application of existing rules
paragraphs.text: Drawing up sectoral codes of conduct, drawn up by the professional federations and supervised by the competent authorities, in order to reflect the principles of loyalty to the realities of each market, is also an option to be explored in order to avoid recourse to legislation.

section_path: 3- strengthening support for professionals to enable better application of existing rules
paragraphs.text: The development of practical guidance within the Consumer Protection Cooperation (CPC) network could also be considered.

section_path: 3- strengthening support for professionals to enable better application of existing rules
paragraphs.text: Similarly, the establishment of a database of decisions by national supervisory authorities and courts would contribute to the harmonisation of interpretations and practices across the EU, notably in the application of Article 25 DSA, while providing platforms with concrete examples to prevent the design of deceptive interfaces and, more generally, unfair practices in the digital environment.

section_path: 3- strengthening support for professionals to enable better application of existing rules
paragraphs.text: Finally, in order to ensure effective European coherence, it is essential to set up a dedicated European coordination body to embody a strong political will, with a clear mandate and specific means to harmonise principles between Member States, promote best practices, while strengthening the capacity of the CPC network in order to enable unified and robust action against global actors, in particular those outside the EU.

section_path: 3- strengthening support for professionals to enable better application of existing rules
